---
title: "Modelling Diabetes Health Indicators"
subtitle: "MA7442 Modelling Data — Assessment 1 (Task 2)"
author: "Shahmeer Shahzad"
date: "2026-03-02"
format:
  pdf:
    geometry:
      - top=2cm
      - bottom=2cm
      - left=1.5cm
      - right=1.5cm
    documentclass: article
    df-print: kable
    mainfont: "Arial"
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r}
#| label: Loading_Packages
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Packages
library(tidymodels)
library(tidyverse)
library(skimr)
library(scales)
library(patchwork)
library(viridis)
library(workflowsets)
library(rcompanion)
library(themis)
library(ranger)
library(vip)
library(shapviz)
library(knitr)
library(probably)
library(conflicted)
library(doParallel)
library(pscl)
library(lmtest)
library(marginaleffects)   
library(ggeffects)
library(MASS)

tidymodels_prefer(quiet = TRUE)
conflicts_prefer(yardstick::accuracy)

```

```{r}
#| label: plot-customise
#| include: false

# Plot format and dimension
knitr::opts_chunk$set(dev = "cairo_pdf")
knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.width = 14, fig.height = 7)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)

# Set global ggplot theme
theme_set(
  theme_classic(base_size = 16) +
    theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
      axis.title    = element_text(size = 18),
      axis.text     = element_text(size = 14),
      legend.title  = element_text(size = 13),
      legend.text   = element_text(size = 12),
      strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
      legend.background = element_rect(
        colour    = "black",
        fill      = "white",
        linewidth = 0.5
      )
    )
)
```

```{r}
#| label: palettes-used
#| include: false
pal_sex <- c(
  "Female" = "orange", 
  "Male"   = "darkgreen"   
)

pal_binary <- c(
  "No"  = "#BBBBBB",  
  "Yes" = "#882255"  
)

pal_genhlth <- c(
  "Excellent" = "#117733",  
  "Very good" = "#44AA99",  
  "Good"      = "#DDCC77",  
  "Fair"      = "#EE8866",  
  "Poor"      = "#CC6677"   
)
```

# 1. Introduction

This report investigates the multidomain determinants of chronic disease
outcomes using the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
Diabetes Health Indicators dataset, which comprises 253,680 respondents
and captures demographic, socioeconomic, behavioural, and
cardiometabolic risk factors across the United States population. Three
distinct but interconnected health outcomes are examined: diabetes
status (no diabetes, prediabetes, diabetes), cardiovascular disease
burden, and mental health burden. The dataset's
population-representative structure and inclusion of both modifiable
risk factors and social determinants make it well-suited for
investigating how clinical risk accumulates within socioeconomic
contexts. The analysis applies diverse modelling approaches tailored to
each outcome type—multinomial classification, binary logistic modelling
with interaction terms, and count regression—to demonstrate that chronic
disease risk is shaped not only by physiological indicators but also by
the structural conditions within which these risks emerge and cluster.

## 1.1 Context and Motivation

Chronic disease outcomes rarely emerge from isolated risk factors.
Rather, they reflect the accumulation and interaction of physiological
vulnerabilities, behavioural choices, and structural determinants across
the lifecourse. Understanding how these domains combine to shape health
outcomes requires analytic frameworks that can accommodate different
outcome types and capture both main effects and moderation.

This report addresses three interconnected questions that together
illustrate the multidimensional patterning of chronic disease risk. The
first examines diabetes status as a multiclass outcome, evaluating which
factors distinguish prediabetes from diabetes and healthy status. The
second investigates whether socioeconomic position moderates the
relationship between clinical risk factors and cardiovascular disease,
testing whether physiological risks operate uniformly across social
strata or are amplified or buffered by income and education. The third
shifts focus to mental health burden as a count outcome, examining how
socioeconomic and lifestyle factors shape the number of mentally
unhealthy days reported in the past month.

Together, these questions demonstrate that health outcomes are embedded
within broader social and behavioural contexts. The analytic
contribution of this report lies in its application of methodologically
diverse approaches—each tailored to the structure of the outcome—while
maintaining a coherent focus on the interconnected nature of chronic
disease determinants. Rather than treating each outcome in isolation,
the report illustrates how different facets of health risk are
structured by common underlying gradients in age, socioeconomic
position, and behavioural clustering.

## 1.2 Reflection on Task 1 Feedback

Three areas of feedback informed the revised analytical approach.

**Scope of the EDA.**\
Task 1 examined associations solely in relation to diabetes status,
constraining the diversity of subsequent research questions. Task 2
introduces comprehensive EDA examining cardiovascular indicators,
general health measures, and behavioural variables as standalone
outcomes.

**Research question overlap.**\
Because the initial EDA centred exclusively on diabetes, the research
questions were conceptually similar. In response, RQ1 has been refined,
the previous RQ2 and RQ3 consolidated, and two new non-diabetes research
questions introduced based on the expanded EDA.

**Figure line type.**\
Task 1 used `geom_line` to connect mean values across ordered
categorical age groups, implying unjustified continuity. Age-structured
visualisations in Task 2 use `geom_col` or point-based representations
that preserve ordinal structure without assuming linear progression.

## 1.3 Overview of Research Questions

The report addresses three research questions that span different
outcome types and analytical objectives:

**RQ1: Multidomain Determinants and Prediction of Diabetes Status**\
Examines diabetes status (no diabetes, prediabetes, diabetes) as a
multiclass outcome. Evaluates which demographic, socioeconomic,
behavioural, and cardiometabolic factors distinguish between diabetes
categories and which modelling approaches (multinomial logistic
regression, random forest, XGBoost) best capture these distinctions.

**RQ2: The Impact of Socioeconomic Moderation on Cardiovascular Risk**\
Investigates whether socioeconomic position (income and education)
moderates the relationship between clinical risk factors (high blood
pressure, high cholesterol, BMI) and cardiovascular disease. Tests
whether physiological risks operate uniformly across social strata or
are amplified or buffered by structural determinants.

**RQ3: Socioeconomic and Lifestyle Determinants of Mental Health
Burden**\
Models the number of self-reported mentally unhealthy days in the past
month as a count outcome. Examines how socioeconomic position, physical
activity, smoking, and physical health burden shape mental health
outcomes, with particular attention to whether protective associations
differ across socioeconomic strata.

Together, these questions demonstrate that chronic disease risk is
multidimensional, requiring analytical approaches tailored to outcome
structure while maintaining focus on the interconnected roles of
clinical indicators, behavioural factors, and social determinants.

## 1.4 Analytical Approach

The diversity of outcome types across the three research questions
necessitates distinct modelling strategies, each tailored to the
structure and distributional properties of the dependent variable.

For **RQ1** (diabetes status), the multiclass categorical outcome
requires methods capable of distinguishing between three unordered
categories. Multinomial logistic regression provides interpretable
baseline estimates of adjusted associations, while tree-based ensemble
methods (random forest and XGBoost) allow flexible capture of non-linear
relationships and variable interactions. Model performance is evaluated
using macro F1-score to account for class imbalance, and interpretation
draws on both regression coefficients and SHAP values to identify
dominant predictors.

For **RQ2** (cardiovascular disease), the binary outcome is modelled
using logistic regression with explicit interaction terms between
socioeconomic variables (income, education) and clinical risk factors
(high blood pressure, high cholesterol, BMI). This approach tests
whether the effect of physiological risk varies systematically across
socioeconomic strata. Predicted probabilities and marginal effects are
computed to illustrate moderation patterns.

For **RQ3** (mental health burden), the count-based outcome (number of
mentally unhealthy days) exhibits overdispersion and excess zeros,
making negative binomial regression or zero-inflated models appropriate.
The analysis evaluates whether socioeconomic and lifestyle factors
influence both the likelihood of reporting any mentally unhealthy days
and the intensity of burden among those affected.

This methodological diversity reflects the principle that analytic
strategy should align with outcome structure and research objectives,
rather than applying a single approach uniformly across all questions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 2. Data Preparation

The dataset and all variable recoding steps are identical to those
described in Task 1 and are reproduced in the setup chunk below (Hidden)
to ensure a fully self-contained, reproducible document. No additional
transformations are required for the EDA in Section 3. All 253,680
records are retained; no missing values were present.

```{r}
#| label: data-import-and-recode
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

diab <- read_csv("dataset1.csv")

diab <- diab |>
  mutate(
    Diabetes = factor(
      Diabetes_012,
      levels = c(0, 1, 2),
      labels = c("No diabetes", "Prediabetes", "Diabetes")
    ),
    across(
      c(CholCheck, Smoker, Stroke, HeartDiseaseorAttack,
        PhysActivity, Fruits, Veggies, HvyAlcoholConsump,
        AnyHealthcare, NoDocbcCost, DiffWalk),
      function(x) factor(x, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("No", "Yes"))
    ),
    across(
      c(HighBP, HighChol),
      function(x) factor(x, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Not High", "High"))
    ),
    Sex = factor(Sex, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Female", "Male")),
    GenHlth = factor(
      GenHlth,
      levels  = 1:5,
      labels  = c("Excellent", "Very good", "Good", "Fair", "Poor"),
      ordered = TRUE
    ),
    Age = factor(
      Age,
      levels  = 1:13,
      labels  = c("18–24", "25–29", "30–34", "35–39", "40–44",
                  "45–49", "50–54", "55–59", "60–64", "65–69",
                  "70–74", "75–79", "80+"),
      ordered = TRUE
    ),
    Education = factor(
      Education,
      levels  = 1:6,
      labels  = c("Kindergarten or less", "Grades 1–8",
                  "Grades 9–11", "High school graduate",
                  "Some college / technical", "College graduate"),
      ordered = TRUE
    ),
    Income = factor(
      Income,
      levels  = 1:8,
      labels  = c("< $10k", "$10k–$14k", "$15k–$19k", "$20k–$24k",
                  "$25k–$34k", "$35k–$49k", "$50k–$74k", "$75k+"),
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  ) |>
  select(-Diabetes_012)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 3. Additional Exploratory Data Analysis: Non-Diabetes Outcomes

The following analyses explore relationships among health, lifestyle,
and socioeconomic variables *independently of diabetes status*. The goal
is to characterise the joint structure of these variables, in particular
to understand what shapes self-reported general health, mental health
burden, and functional limitation, and to motivate the non-diabetes
research questions introduced in Section 4.

## 3.1 Cardiovascular Risk Indicators: Demographic, Socioeconomic and Behavioural Gradients

This section explores the population structure and correlates of key
cardiovascular risk indicators, namely high blood pressure, high
cholesterol, and stroke. The analysis moves beyond diabetes to examine
how these outcomes are patterned across age, sex, socioeconomic
position, and behavioural factors. Emphasis is placed on identifying
demographic concentration, socioeconomic gradients, and clustering with
related health behaviours. The objective is to establish structural
regularities in the data that may inform subsequent modelling and risk
interpretation.

```{r}
#| label: age-sex-pyramid-prep
#| include: false

bp_pyramid <- diab |>
  count(HighBP, Age, Sex) |>
  group_by(HighBP, Sex) |>
  mutate(
    percent = 100 * n / sum(n)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    percent_signed = case_when(
      Sex == "Male"   ~ -percent,
      Sex == "Female" ~  percent,
      TRUE            ~ NA_real_
    )
  )

chol_pyramid <- diab |>
  count(HighChol, Age, Sex) |>
  group_by(HighChol, Sex) |>
  mutate(
    percent = 100 * n / sum(n)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    percent_signed = case_when(
      Sex == "Male"   ~ -percent,
      Sex == "Female" ~  percent,
      TRUE            ~ NA_real_))

stroke_pyramid <- diab |>
  filter(!is.na(Stroke), !is.na(Age), !is.na(Sex)) |>
  count(Stroke, Age, Sex) |>
  group_by(Stroke, Sex) |>
  mutate(
    percent = 100 * n / sum(n)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    percent_signed = case_when(
      Sex == "Male"   ~ -percent,
      Sex == "Female" ~  percent,
      TRUE            ~ NA_real_
    )
  )

```

### 3.1.1 Age–Sex Distribution by High Blood Pressure Status

To understand how cardiovascular risk is demographically structured, the
age and sex distribution of individuals with and without high blood
pressure is examined first. This establishes whether cardiovascular
burden is concentrated in specific demographic subgroups or distributed
broadly across the population.

```{r}
#| label: highbp-age-sex-pyramid
#| fig.height: 7
#| fig.width: 14

ggplot(bp_pyramid,
       aes(x = Age, y = percent_signed, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.9, colour = "white") +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ HighBP, nrow = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(abs(x), "%")
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = pal_sex
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Age–sex distribution by high blood pressure status",
    x     = "Age group",
    y     = "Percent within blood pressure group",
    fill  = "Sex"
  )
```

### 3.1.2 Age–sex distribution by **high cholesterol** status

The age and sex distribution is examined for high cholesterol status to
assess whether demographic patterning is similar to that observed for
high blood pressure or whether distinct age-sex profiles emerge.

```{r}
#| label: highchol-age-sex-pyramid
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 14

ggplot(chol_pyramid,
       aes(x = Age, y = percent_signed, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.9, colour = "white") +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ HighChol, nrow = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(abs(x), "%")
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = pal_sex
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Age–sex distribution by high cholesterol status",
    x     = "Age group",
    y     = "Percent within cholesterol group",
    fill  = "Sex")
```

### 3.1.3 Age–sex distribution by heart disease history

Heart disease or myocardial infarction represents a more severe
cardiovascular outcome than hypertension or hypercholesterolaemia. The
demographic distribution is examined to determine whether heart disease
exhibits a similar age-sex structure or shows more pronounced
concentration in specific groups.

```{r}
#| label: hrtdis-age-sex-pyramid
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 14

hrt_dis_pyramid <- diab |>
  count(HeartDiseaseorAttack, Age, Sex) |>
  group_by(HeartDiseaseorAttack, Sex) |>
  mutate(
    percent = 100 * n / sum(n)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    percent_signed = case_when(
      Sex == "Male"   ~ -percent,
      Sex == "Female" ~  percent,
      TRUE            ~ NA_real_
    )
  )

ggplot(hrt_dis_pyramid,
       aes(x = Age, y = percent_signed, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.9, colour = "white") +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ HeartDiseaseorAttack, nrow = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(abs(x), "%")
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = pal_sex
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Age–sex distribution by Heart Disease/Attack history",
    x     = "Age group",
    y     = "Percent within Heart Disease history group",
    fill  = "Sex"
  )
```

> Across all three outcomes, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and
> stroke, the dominant structural feature is age concentration. Each
> “Yes” panel is heavily skewed toward the 55–69 and older age bands,
> while representation below age 40 is minimal. This confirms that
> cardiovascular burden in this dataset is fundamentally age-structured
> rather than evenly distributed across the life course.
>
> However, the degree of age concentration differs meaningfully between
> outcomes. High blood pressure and high cholesterol show similar
> mid-to-late life clustering, with substantial presence beginning in
> the 50–54 band and peaking around 60–69. Stroke, by contrast, exhibits
> a far sharper right-skew: the “Yes” group is overwhelmingly
> concentrated in older age bands, with negligible representation in
> younger adults. This suggests stroke is not merely age-associated but
> strongly age-dependent relative to the other indicators.
>
> Sex differences are comparatively modest within age strata for high
> blood pressure and high cholesterol. Once age is conditioned upon,
> male–female distributions appear broadly parallel, suggesting that sex
> operates as a secondary structuring factor relative to age. In the
> heart disease group, however, there is a slight female predominance in
> the oldest age bands, which may reflect differential survival into
> advanced age rather than a pure sex-specific risk gradient.
>
> Importantly, these pyramids depict the **composition within outcome
> groups**, not prevalence across the population. They therefore
> highlight demographic concentration rather than risk probability. The
> consistency across high blood pressure and high cholesterol suggests
> shared cardiometabolic ageing processes, whereas heart disease
> displays a more extreme demographic compression, indicating it may
> represent the culmination of accumulated vascular risk rather than an
> early-stage cardiovascular condition.
>
> Taken together, the figures indicate that age is the primary axis of
> cardiovascular risk patterning in this sample. Any socioeconomic or
> behavioural gradients observed later must therefore be interpreted
> within the context of this strong underlying demographic structure.

```{r}
#| label: ses-heatmap
#| include: false

bp_ses <- diab |>
  mutate(
    bp_binary = if_else(HighBP == "High", 1, 0)) |>
  group_by(Education, Income) |>
  summarise(prev_bp = mean(bp_binary),
            n = n(),
    .groups = "drop")

chol_ses <- diab |>
  mutate(chol_bin = if_else(HighChol == "High", 1, 0)) |>
  group_by(Education, Income) |>
  summarise(
    prev_chol = mean(chol_bin),
    n = n(),
    .groups = "drop")

stroke_ses <- diab |>
  mutate(
    heart_d_bin = if_else(HeartDiseaseorAttack == "Yes", 1, 0)) |>
  group_by(Education, Income) |>
  summarise(prev_heartdis = mean(heart_d_bin),n = n(),
    .groups = "drop")
```

### 3.1.4 Joint SES heatmap of High Blood Pressure prevalence

To examine whether socioeconomic position structures cardiovascular risk
independently of demographic factors, prevalence is calculated across
all combinations of education and income categories. This
two-dimensional approach reveals whether education and income operate as
distinct gradients or merely proxy for one another.

```{r}
#| label: highbp-ses-heatmap
#| fig.height: 8
#| fig.width: 15

bp_ses |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Income, y = Education, fill = prev_bp)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(prev_bp, accuracy = 1)),
    colour   = "white",
    fontface = "bold",
    size     = 4.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low  = "#F1EEF6",
    high = "#882255",
    name = "Prevalence\nHigh BP",
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Prevalence of high blood pressure by education and income",
    x     = "Income band",
    y     = "Education level")
```

### 3.1.5 Joint SES heatmap of High Cholesterol prevalence

The same socioeconomic analysis is applied to high cholesterol to assess
whether the patterning differs from hypertension or follows a similar
structure.

```{r}
#| label: highchol-ses-heatmap
#| fig.height: 8
#| fig.width: 15

chol_ses |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Income, y = Education, fill = prev_chol)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(prev_chol, accuracy = 1)),
    colour   = "white",
    fontface = "bold",
    size     = 3.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low    = "#F1EEF6",      
    high   = "#882255",      
    name   = "Prevalence\nHigh cholesterol",
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Prevalence of high cholesterol by education and income",
    x     = "Income band",
    y     = "Education level"
  )
```

### 3.1.6 Joint SES heatmap of Heart Disease prevalence

Finally, heart disease prevalence is examined across the same
socioeconomic grid. Given the lower absolute prevalence of heart
disease, this analysis assesses whether socioeconomic gradients remain
detectable or become obscured by smaller cell counts.

```{r}
#| label: heartdis-ses-heatmap
#| fig.height: 8
#| fig.width: 15

stroke_ses |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Income, y = Education, fill = prev_heartdis)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(prev_heartdis, accuracy = 1)),
    colour = "white",
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#F1EEF6",      
    high = "#882255",
    name = "Prevalence\nHeart Disease history",
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Prevalence of Heart Attack history by education and income",
        x = "Income band",
        y = "Education level")
```

> Across all three outcomes, a clear socioeconomic gradient is evident.
> Prevalence is consistently highest in the lowest education and lowest
> income strata and declines with increasing educational attainment and
> income. The lower-left cells (least educated, lowest income) exhibit
> the greatest burden, while college graduates in the highest income
> bands show the lowest prevalence. This pattern indicates cumulative
> socioeconomic disadvantage rather than a single-dimension effect.
>
> High blood pressure and high cholesterol display particularly strong
> and stable two-dimensional gradients. Within nearly every education
> category, prevalence decreases progressively as income rises.
> Likewise, within each income band, higher education corresponds to
> lower prevalence. The monotonic pattern across both axes suggests that
> both income and education independently structure cardiometabolic
> risk, rather than one merely proxying for the other.
>
> Heart disease exhibits the same directional gradient but at
> substantially lower absolute prevalence and with more variability
> across cells. Some low-education, low-income cells show markedly
> elevated heart disease prevalence (e.g., 20-27%), while higher SES
> cells approach very low levels. The noisier pattern likely reflects
> smaller counts and the relative rarity of heart disease compared with
> hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia, but the overall concentration
> in socioeconomically disadvantaged groups remains clear.
>
> Importantly, these are crude prevalence estimates. As shown in the
> age–sex pyramids, lower socioeconomic strata contain
> disproportionately older individuals, and age is a dominant
> determinant of all three outcomes. Therefore, part of the observed SES
> gradient likely reflects demographic composition. However, the
> persistence of a consistent gradient across both income and education
> dimensions suggests that socioeconomic position is not merely a proxy
> for age but a meaningful structural correlate of cardiovascular
> burden.

### 3.1.7 Prevalence of cardiovascular outcomes by accumulated risk factors

To assess whether cardiometabolic risk factors operate independently or
cumulatively, cardiovascular prevalence is examined across groups
defined by the number of concurrent risk factors present (obesity,
physical inactivity, smoking, and diabetes). This establishes whether
the joint burden of multiple risk exposures amplifies cardiovascular
risk beyond what would be expected from isolated factors.

```{r}
#| label: cvd-obesity-physact-prep
#| include: false
diab_risk <- diab |>
  mutate(
    Obese  = if_else(BMI >= 30, 1L, 0L),
    Inactive = if_else(PhysActivity == "No", 1L, 0L),
    Smoker_bin = if_else(Smoker == "Yes", 1L, 0L),
    Diabetes_bin = if_else(Diabetes != "No diabetes", 1L, 0L),
    risk_count = Obese + Inactive + Smoker_bin + Diabetes_bin,
    risk_group = case_when(
      risk_count == 0 ~ "0",
      risk_count == 1 ~ "1",
      risk_count == 2 ~ "2",
      risk_count == 3 ~ "3",
      risk_count >= 4 ~ "4+"
    ),
    risk_group = factor(risk_group, levels = c("0","1","2","3","4+"))
  )

cvd_long <- diab_risk |>
  select(risk_group, HighBP, HighChol, HeartDiseaseorAttack) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(HighBP, HighChol, HeartDiseaseorAttack),
    names_to = "Outcome",
    values_to = "Status"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    outcome_event = case_when(
      Outcome == "HighBP" ~ Status == "High",
      Outcome == "HighChol" ~ Status == "High",
      Outcome == "HeartDiseaseorAttack" ~ Status == "Yes",
      TRUE ~ NA
    ),
    Outcome = recode(
      Outcome,
      HighBP = "High blood pressure",
      HighChol = "High cholesterol",
      HeartDiseaseorAttack = "Heart Disease or Attack history")) |>
  group_by(Outcome, risk_group) |>
  summarise(
    prop = mean(outcome_event, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop")
```

```{r}
#| label: cvd-obesity-physact
#| fig.height: 6
#| fig.width: 14


ggplot(cvd_long, aes(x = risk_group, y = prop, fill = risk_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ Outcome, nrow = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, 1)
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Purples", name = "Risk\ncount") +
  labs(
    title = "Prevalence of cardiovascular outcomes by cardiometabolic risk factor count",
    x     = "Number of risk factors present\n(obesity, inactivity, smoking, diabetes)",
    y     = "Proportion with outcome"
  )
```

> Cardiovascular outcomes show a clear **dose–response relationship with
> the accumulation of cardiometabolic risk factors**. For both **high
> blood pressure** and **high cholesterol**, prevalence increases
> steadily as additional risk factors are present. Individuals with no
> risk factors show relatively low prevalence (around one quarter to one
> third), while those with **four or more risk factors exhibit very high
> prevalence**, exceeding roughly **70–80%** for both conditions. This
> pattern indicates that these outcomes are strongly associated with the
> combined burden of obesity, physical inactivity, smoking, and
> diabetes.
>
> **Heart disease or attack history follows the same upward trend**, but
> at substantially lower levels overall. Even among individuals with
> multiple risk factors, heart disease prevalence remains below roughly
> **25–30%**, reflecting the relative rarity of this severe outcome
> compared with hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia in the
> population.
>
> Overall, the figure demonstrates that **cardiometabolic risk factors
> act cumulatively rather than independently**. As risk exposures
> accumulate, the likelihood of cardiovascular conditions, particularly
> hypertension and high cholesterol, increases markedly. This cumulative
> patterning suggests that multivariable models examining cardiovascular
> outcomes should account for the joint burden of metabolic and
> behavioural risk factors rather than treating them as isolated
> predictors.

### 3.1.8 Joint blood pressure and cholesterol profiles across age groups

Having established that both high blood pressure and high cholesterol
exhibit strong age gradients and socioeconomic patterning, this analysis
examines whether these two conditions occur independently or cluster
within individuals as they age. Understanding whether dual elevation
emerges as a distinct profile or merely reflects independent
accumulation of separate risks informs whether cardiovascular modelling
should treat these as additive effects or interacting pathways.

```{r}
#| label: highchol-highbp-age-prep
#| include: false

bp_chol_age <- diab |>
  count(Age, HighBP, HighChol) |>
  group_by(Age) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup()

```

```{r}
#| label: highchol-highbp-age
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6

ggplot(bp_chol_age,
       aes(x = Age, y = prop, fill = interaction(HighBP, HighChol,
                                                  sep = " / Chol: "))) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", width = 0.85) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D", direction = -1) +
  labs(
    title = "Joint blood pressure and cholesterol status by age group",
    x     = "Age group",
    y     = "Proportion within age group",
    fill  = "BP / Cholesterol"
  ) 
```

> The distribution of cardiovascular profiles shifts systematically with
> age. Younger groups are overwhelmingly characterised by normal blood
> pressure and cholesterol, whereas the proportion with dual elevation
> (high/high) increases steadily from midlife onward and becomes the
> largest single cardiovascular profile in older age bands, representing
> approximately 40% of individuals aged 70+. This pattern reflects
> progressive cardiometabolic accumulation rather than isolated risk
> emergence.
>
> Importantly, the high/high category expands more rapidly than either
> single-risk category, indicating clustering rather than independence
> of these conditions. By ages 65+, dual dysregulation accounts for a
> substantial share of the population within each age group, suggesting
> convergence of vascular risk factors with ageing.
>
> The pattern implies that hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia are
> not parallel but interacting processes within cardiovascular ageing,
> reinforcing their role as intermediate markers in the pathway toward
> more severe vascular outcomes. For cardiovascular modelling, this
> clustering suggests that interaction terms between blood pressure and
> cholesterol status may better capture risk than treating them as
> independent additive effects.

### 3.1.9 Co-occurrence of Hypertension and High Cholesterol

The previous analysis demonstrated that high blood pressure and high
cholesterol cluster within age groups more than would be expected if
they occurred independently. To quantify the overall population-level
co-occurrence, this section examines the joint prevalence of both
conditions across all respondents. This establishes the baseline
distribution of cardiovascular risk profiles and determines whether dual
elevation represents a distinct subgroup or simply reflects overlapping
but independent conditions.

```{r}
#| label: highbp-highchol-joint
#| fig.height: 8
#| fig.width: 12

diab |>
  count(HighBP, HighChol) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = HighBP, y = HighChol, fill = prop)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(prop,accuracy = 0.1)),
    size     = 6,
    fontface = "bold",
    colour   = "white"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Joint prevalence of high blood pressure and high cholesterol",
    x     = "High blood pressure",
    y     = "High cholesterol"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = 'none'
  )
```

> Approximately **40.2%** of the sample has neither high blood pressure
> nor high cholesterol. The remaining **59.8%** have at least one of the
> two conditions, indicating that cardiometabolic risk is common in this
> population.
>
> The largest single elevated profile is **concurrent high blood
> pressure and high cholesterol (25.5%)**, which exceeds either isolated
> condition (17.4% BP only; 16.9% cholesterol only). This suggests
> meaningful clustering rather than independent occurrence of the two
> risk factors.
>
> Overall, hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia appear strongly
> co-patterned, with dual elevation more common than either
> single-condition state, reinforcing their joint role in cardiovascular
> risk stratification. This co-occurrence pattern supports modelling
> strategies that account for interaction effects between blood pressure
> and cholesterol when predicting more severe cardiovascular outcomes,
> rather than assuming their effects are purely additive.

### 3.1.10 Heart disease prevalence by blood pressure and cholesterol status

Having established that high blood pressure and high cholesterol
co-occur more frequently than would be expected under independence, this
section examines whether their joint presence is associated with
elevated risk of more severe cardiovascular outcomes. Specifically,
heart disease or myocardial infarction prevalence is compared across the
four BP/cholesterol profiles to test whether dual elevation represents a
meaningful risk amplification.

```{r}
#| label: stroke-bp-chol-prep
#| include: false
heart_bpchol <- diab |>
  count(HighBP, HighChol, HeartDiseaseorAttack) |>
  group_by(HighBP, HighChol) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(HeartDiseaseorAttack == "Yes") |>
  mutate(
    BP_Chol = paste0("BP: ", HighBP, "\nChol: ", HighChol)
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: hrt-bp-chol
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 7

ggplot(heart_bpchol,
       aes(x = BP_Chol, y = prop, fill = prop)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)),
    vjust = -0.4,
    size = 5.5,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
  labels = percent_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "C", direction = -1, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Heart disease or attack prevalence by blood pressure and cholesterol status",
    x     = "Blood pressure / cholesterol profile",
    y     = "Proportion with heart disease or attack history"
  )
```

> Heart disease prevalence increases monotonically across cardiovascular
> profiles. Among individuals with neither high blood pressure nor high
> cholesterol, heart disease prevalence is approximately **2.8%**. This
> rises to **7.2%** with isolated high cholesterol, **9.7%** with
> isolated high blood pressure, and **21.1%** among those with both
> conditions elevated. The prevalence in the dual-high group is more
> than double that observed with isolated high blood pressure,
> suggesting amplification rather than simple addition of risks.
>
> The gradient indicates cumulative vascular burden, with blood pressure
> appearing more strongly associated with heart disease than cholesterol
> alone. The sharp elevation in the dual-high group suggests that
> interaction effects between blood pressure and cholesterol may better
> capture cardiovascular risk than treating them as independent additive
> predictors. This pattern motivates the inclusion of BP×cholesterol
> interaction terms in subsequent cardiovascular disease modelling
> (RQ2).

### 3.1.11 Joint co-occurrence profiles of high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and stroke

```{r}
#| label: joint-prep
#| include: false

joint <- diab |>
  summarise(
    `HighBP & HighChol` = mean(HighBP == "High" & HighChol == "High"),
    `HighBP & HeartDisease` = mean(HighBP == "High" & HeartDiseaseorAttack == "Yes"),
    `HighChol & HeartDisease` = mean(HighChol == "High" & HeartDiseaseorAttack == "Yes"),
    `HighBP only` = mean(
      HighBP == "High" &
      HighChol != "High" &
      HeartDiseaseorAttack != "Yes"
    ),
    `HighChol only` = mean(
      HighChol == "High" &
      HighBP != "High" &
      HeartDiseaseorAttack != "Yes"
    ),
    `HeartDisease only` = mean(
      HeartDiseaseorAttack == "Yes" &
      HighBP != "High" &
      HighChol != "High"
    ),
    `All three` = mean(
      HighBP == "High" &
      HighChol == "High" &
      HeartDiseaseorAttack == "Yes"
    )
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Profile", values_to = "Proportion")

max_prop <- max(joint$Proportion)
```

```{r}
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 14

ggplot(joint, aes(x = reorder(Profile, Proportion), y = Proportion, fill = Proportion)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(Proportion, accuracy = 0.1)),
    hjust = -0.15,
    size = 4.5
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_x_discrete(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.05))) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, max_prop * 1.15)
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "D", direction = -1, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Co-occurrence profiles for HighBP, HighChol, and heart disease/attack history",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Proportion of full sample"
  )
```

### 3.1.12 Association Strength - Cramér's V

To identify which demographic, socioeconomic, behavioural, and health
factors show the strongest associations with cardiovascular outcomes,
Cramér's V is calculated for each predictor-outcome pair. This
effect-size measure (ranging 0-1) quantifies association strength for
categorical variables, enabling direct comparison across predictors with
different scales and distributions. The analysis establishes which
factors warrant prioritisation in subsequent modelling and whether
different cardiovascular outcomes share common predictive structures or
require distinct variable selection strategies.

```{r}
#| label: cvd-binary-indicators-prep
#| include: false
nondm_outcomes <- c("HighBP", "HighChol", "HeartDiseaseorAttack")

pred_vars <- c("Diabetes", "Age", "Sex", "Income", "Education",
               "PhysActivity", "Smoker", "BMI_cat",
               "DiffWalk", "Stroke",
               "AnyHealthcare", "NoDocbcCost",
               "GenHlth", "HvyAlcoholConsump")

diab_cv <- diab |>
  mutate(
    BMI_cat = cut(
      BMI,
      breaks = c(0, 18.5, 25, 30, Inf),
      labels = c("Underweight", "Normal", "Overweight", "Obese"),
      right = FALSE
    )
  )

cv_nondm <- map_dfr(nondm_outcomes, function(outcome) {
  map_dfr(pred_vars, function(pred) {
    v <- cramerV(table(diab_cv[[outcome]], diab_cv[[pred]], useNA = "no"))
    tibble(
      Outcome = outcome,
      Predictor = pred,
      CramersV = as.numeric(v)
    )
  })
}) |>
  mutate(
    Outcome = recode(
      Outcome,
      HighBP = "High Blood Pressure",
      HighChol = "High Cholesterol",
      HeartDiseaseorAttack = "Heart Disease/Attack"
    )
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: cvd-binary-indicators
#| fig-height: 9
#| fig-width: 14
ggplot(cv_nondm,aes(x = reorder(Predictor, CramersV),
                    y = CramersV,
                    fill = Outcome)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
           width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(CramersV, 2)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
            hjust = -0.1,
            size = 3.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  expand_limits(y = max(cv_nondm$CramersV) + 0.03) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "High Blood Pressure" = "#2c7bb6",
      "High Cholesterol" = "#f46d43",
      "Heart Disease/Attack" = "#1a9641")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.45)) +
  labs(
    title = "Association strength with Binary outcomes (Cramér's V)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Cramér's V",
    fill = "Outcome") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

> This chart highlights how different risk factors weigh against
> cardiovascular outcomes using **Cramér’s V** (a measure of association
> where higher values indicate a stronger relationship).
>
> The data reveals two distinct "profiles" of disease:
>
> 1.  **Metabolic & Age-Driven (High BP & Cholesterol)**
>
> Hypertension and High Cholesterol show strongest associations with
> **systemic, long-term factors**.
>
> -   **Primary Associations:** **Age** ($V \approx 0.35$) and **General
>     Health** ($V \approx 0.30$) exhibit the strongest relationships.
>
> -   **Secondary Associations:** Notable links to **Diabetes** and
>     **BMI**, consistent with their roles as markers of general
>     metabolic dysregulation.
>
> -   **Takeaway:** These conditions follow a broad, population-wide
>     risk gradient structured by age and overall physical state,
>     indicating that age will likely dominate as a predictor in
>     multivariable models.
>
> 2.  **Clinical & Pathological (Heart Disease/Attack)**
>
> Heart disease or attack history follows a different logic, focusing
> more on **existing cardiovascular damage** than gradual metabolic
> ageing alone.
>
> -   **Primary Drivers:** **General Health** ($V \approx 0.27$) and
>     **Diabetes** ($V \approx 0.27$) are strongest, followed by **Age**
>     ($V \approx 0.23$), **Walking Difficulty** ($V \approx 0.21$), and
>     **Stroke history** ($V \approx 0.20$).
>
> -   **Key Difference:** Age is significantly less dominant for heart
>     disease than for blood pressure or cholesterol. BMI shows almost
>     no association with heart disease ($V \approx 0.06$) despite
>     moderate associations with the other outcomes.
>
> -   **Takeaway:** Heart disease appears as a **clinically acute
>     endpoint**, most strongly associated with poor general health,
>     diabetes, and functional impairment rather than age-gradient
>     metabolic factors alone. This suggests interaction effects warrant
>     investigation in subsequent cardiovascular modelling (RQ2).

|                          |                         |                       |
|--------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Outcome**              | **Strongest Predictor** | **Second Predictor**  |
| **High Blood Pressure**  | Age (0.35)              | General Health (0.30) |
| **High Cholesterol**     | Age (0.29)              | General Health (0.21) |
| **Heart Disease/Attack** | GenHlth/Diabetes (0.27) | Age (0.23)            |

### 3.1.13 Summary of Key Findings

Section 3.1 establishes that cardiovascular risk in this dataset is
structured along three primary axes: **age, socioeconomic position, and
cardiometabolic clustering**.

First, **age is the dominant organising factor**. High blood pressure
and high cholesterol rise steadily from midlife onward, while heart
disease is far more sharply concentrated in older age groups. Heart
disease appears less as a gradual condition and more as a late-stage
vascular outcome.

Second, a **clear and consistent socioeconomic gradient** is evident
across all three outcomes. Lower education and lower income strata show
substantially higher crude prevalence, with monotonic declines across
both dimensions. Although partly influenced by age composition, the
two-dimensional gradient suggests an independent structural role for
socioeconomic position.

Third, **cardiometabolic conditions cluster rather than occur
independently**. Dual high blood pressure and high cholesterol (25.5%)
exceed either isolated condition, and heart disease prevalence increases
monotonically across BP/cholesterol profiles, peaking in the dual-high
group. This supports a cumulative vascular burden framework.

Fourth, **obesity strongly structures hypertension and cholesterol but
not heart disease**, reinforcing the interpretation of BP and
cholesterol as intermediate metabolic states. Heart disease instead
aligns more strongly with diabetes, general health, and walking
difficulty.

Finally, association analyses (Cramér’s V) reveal two distinct disease
profiles:

-   **Hypertension and cholesterol** are predominantly age and
    general-health driven.

-   **Heart disease or myocardial infarction** is more closely
    associated with established poor general health, diabetes, and
    functional impairment.

Overall, Section 3.1 demonstrates that cardiovascular outcomes in this
dataset are not randomly distributed but follow coherent demographic,
socioeconomic, and pathological structures. These findings provide the
empirical foundation for the modelling strategy developed in Section 4.

### 3.1.14 Implications for Modelling

The exploratory analysis of cardiometabolic outcomes suggests several
considerations for subsequent modelling. First, the strong accumulation
pattern observed across cardiometabolic risk factors indicates that
lifestyle and metabolic variables such as obesity, smoking, physical
inactivity, and diabetes are likely to act as key predictors of
cardiovascular outcomes. Second, the association strength analysis shows
that demographic and health-status variables—particularly age and
self-rated general health—have the strongest relationships with high
blood pressure and high cholesterol, indicating that these variables
should be prioritised in predictive models.

For heart disease or myocardial infarction, the strongest associations
appear with general health status, diabetes, and functional limitation
(difficulty walking), suggesting that heart disease risk may be more
closely linked to underlying health status and comorbidity than to
age-gradient metabolic factors alone. The relatively low prevalence of
heart disease compared with hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia
suggests that models predicting heart disease may face class imbalance
and may require approaches suitable for rare outcomes, or alternatively,
that interaction effects between clinical risk factors and socioeconomic
position (as explored in RQ2) may reveal important moderation patterns.

Overall, the EDA indicates that **demographic factors, general health
status, and cardiometabolic risk profiles** are likely to be central
components of the modelling framework used to investigate cardiovascular
outcomes.

## 3.2 Patterns and determinants of mental health burden

This section examines the distribution and key correlates of **mental
health burden**, measured as the number of mentally unhealthy days
reported in the past 30 days. The analysis explores how mental health
burden varies across demographic, socioeconomic, and health-related
factors, including age, physical health, and lifestyle behaviours. By
identifying systematic patterns in mental health outcomes, this section
aims to highlight the factors most strongly associated with elevated
psychological distress in the population.

### 3.2.1 Distribution of mentally unhealthy days in the past 30 days

```{r}
#| label: mentlhlth-distribution
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 14
ggplot(diab, aes(x = MentHlth)) +
  geom_histogram(
    binwidth = 1,
    boundary = 0,
    fill = "steelblue",
    colour = "black"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(0, 30, by = 5),
    limits = c(0, 30)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K"),
    breaks = seq(0,200000,by = 25000)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of mentally unhealthy days in the past 30 days",
    x = "Number of mentally unhealthy days",
    y = "Frequency") 
```

> The distribution of mentally unhealthy days is **highly
> right-skewed**, with a very large proportion of respondents reporting
> **zero mentally unhealthy days in the past month**. Frequencies
> decline sharply as the number of unhealthy days increases, producing a
> long right tail that extends toward the upper bound of 30 days. This
> pattern indicates that while most individuals report little or no
> recent mental health burden, a smaller subset experiences **frequent
> or persistent distress**.
>
> The distribution also shows evidence of **excess zeros and strong
> dispersion**, with the spike at zero far exceeding the frequencies of
> neighbouring values. Such a structure suggests that standard linear
> modelling would be inappropriate, and motivates the use of
> **count-based models**, potentially including **zero-inflated or
> overdispersed models (e.g., Poisson or zero-inflated negative
> binomial)** when analysing mental health burden as an outcome in
> subsequent modelling.

### 3.2.3 Mental Health Burden by Income and Sex

```{r}
#| label: mental-health-income
#| fig.height: 7
#| fig.width: 14
diab |>
  mutate(HighMH = MentHlth >= 14) |>
  group_by(Income, Sex) |>
  summarise(prop_high = mean(HighMH), .groups = "drop") |>
  
  ggplot(aes(x = Income, y = prop_high, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
    width = 0.7,
    colour = "black",
    linewidth = 0.2
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(prop_high, accuracy = 1)),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.9),
    vjust = -0.3, size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, NA)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = pal_sex
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Prevalence of high mental health burden (≥14 days) by income and sex",
    x     = "Household income",
    y     = "Proportion with ≥14 mentally unhealthy days",
    fill  = "Sex"
  )
```

> A clear socioeconomic gradient is evident in the prevalence of high
> mental health burden. The proportion of respondents reporting ≥14
> mentally unhealthy days declines steadily as household income
> increases, indicating that individuals in lower-income groups
> experience substantially higher levels of psychological distress. In
> the lowest income category (\<\$10k), roughly 28% of females and 26%
> of males report high mental health burden, compared with only 6% and
> 4% respectively in the highest income group (\$75k+).
>
> Across all income categories, females consistently report higher
> prevalence than males, although the difference between sexes is modest
> relative to the overall income gradient. These results suggest that
> socioeconomic position is a strong determinant of mental health
> burden, with financial disadvantage associated with markedly higher
> levels of frequent psychological distress. (Money doesn't buy
> happiness :) )

### 3.2.3 Mental Health Burden by Educatioand Sex

```{r}
#| label: mental-health-education
#| fig.height: 7
#| fig.width: 14
diab |>
  mutate(HighMH = MentHlth >= 14) |>
  group_by(Education, Sex) |>
  summarise(prop_high = mean(HighMH), .groups = "drop") |>
  
  ggplot(aes(x = Education, y = prop_high, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
    width = 0.7,
    colour = "black",
    linewidth = 0.2
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(prop_high, accuracy = 1)),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.9),
    vjust = -0.3, size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, NA)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = pal_sex
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Prevalence of high mental health burden (≥14 days) by income and sex",
    x     = "Household income",
    y     = "Proportion with ≥14 mentally unhealthy days",
    fill  = "Sex"
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1))
```

> A clear educational gradient is visible in the prevalence of high
> mental health burden. Individuals with lower levels of educational
> attainment report substantially higher proportions of ≥14 mentally
> unhealthy days compared with those with higher education. For example,
> among respondents with kindergarten or less education, approximately
> 22% report high mental health burden, whereas this proportion declines
> to around 8% for females and 5% for males among college graduates.
>
> Across all education categories, females consistently report higher
> levels of mental health burden than males, although the difference
> between sexes is relatively modest compared with the overall education
> gradient. The steady decline in prevalence with increasing educational
> attainment suggests that socioeconomic resources and opportunities
> associated with education may play an important role in mental health
> outcomes.
>
> Overall, the figure indicates that lower educational attainment is
> associated with substantially higher levels of frequent psychological
> distress, reinforcing the broader pattern of socioeconomic
> inequalities in mental health observed in the income analysis.

### 3.2.4 Mental Health Burden by Age Group and Sex

```{r}
#| label: mental-health-age-lollipop
#| fig.height: 7
#| fig.width: 14


mh_age_sex <- diab |>
  group_by(Age, Sex) |>
  summarise(mean_mh = mean(MentHlth, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

pos_dodge <- position_dodge(width = 0.6)

ggplot(mh_age_sex, aes(x = Age, y = mean_mh, colour = Sex)) +
  geom_linerange(
    aes(ymin = 0, ymax = mean_mh),
    position = pos_dodge,
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_point(
    position = pos_dodge,
    size = 3.8
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, NA),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = pal_sex
  ) +
  labs(
    title  = "Mean mentally unhealthy days by age group and sex",
    x      = "Age group",
    y      = "Mean mentally unhealthy days (past 30 days)",
    colour = "Sex"
  ) 
```

> Mean mentally unhealthy days are higher among women than men in every
> age group. The difference is most visible in younger and middle
> adulthood, where women report roughly 1–2 more days on average than
> men.
>
> Across age, the pattern is not monotonic. Mean days are relatively
> high in early adulthood, remain similar through midlife, and then
> decline progressively after around age 60 for both sexes. The lowest
> averages are observed in the oldest age groups.

### 3.2.5 Joint Distribution of Mental and Physical Health Burden by Sex

```{r}
#| label: menth-physh-hexbin
#| fig.height: 7
#| fig.width: 14
ggplot(diab, aes(x = MentHlth, y = PhysHlth)) +
  geom_hex(bins = 30) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", name = "Count", trans = "log10") +
  facet_wrap(~ Sex, nrow = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Joint distribution of mentally and physically unhealthy days by sex",
    x     = "Mentally unhealthy days (past 30)",
    y     = "Physically unhealthy days (past 30)"
  )
```

> In both panels, the highest concentration of observations occurs near
> zero mentally and physically unhealthy days. Density decreases as
> values increase along either axis, and combinations of simultaneously
> high mental and physical burden are relatively sparse.
>
> There is visible clustering along diagonal regions, indicating that
> higher numbers of mentally unhealthy days tend to coincide with higher
> numbers of physically unhealthy days rather than being concentrated
> along a single axis.
>
> The overall distributional shape is similar for women and men, with
> broadly comparable density patterns across low, moderate, and higher
> values.

### 3.2.6 Prevalence of High Mental Health Burden by Physical and Cardiometabolic indicators

```{r}
#| include: false
diab_mh_phys <- diab |>
  mutate(
    HighMH = factor(if_else(MentHlth >= 14, "Yes", "No"),
                    levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    
    Obese = factor(if_else(BMI >= 30, "Yes", "No"),
                    levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    
    BadPhysHlth = factor(if_else(PhysHlth >= 14, "Yes", "No"),
                    levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    
    Diabetes = factor(if_else(Diabetes == "Diabetes", "Yes", "No"),
                        levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    
    HighBP = factor(if_else(HighBP == "High", "Yes", "No"),
                        levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    
    HighChol = factor(if_else(HighChol == "High", "Yes", "No"),
                        levels = c("No", "Yes"))
  ) |>
  select(
    HighMH,
    Obese,
    PhysActivity,
    DiffWalk,
    BadPhysHlth,
    Diabetes,
    HeartDiseaseorAttack,
    HighBP,
    HighChol
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -HighMH,
    names_to = "Indicator",
    values_to = "Status"
  ) |> 
  count(Indicator, Status, HighMH) |>
  group_by(Indicator, Status) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(HighMH == "Yes")
```

```{r}
#| fig.height: 7
#| fig.width: 14

diab_mh_phys |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Status, y = prop, fill = Status)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(prop, accuracy = 1)),
    vjust = -0.3,
    size  = 3.3
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ Indicator, nrow = 2, ncol = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, NA),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.10))
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_binary) +
  labs(
    title = "Prevalence of high Mental Health Burden by Physical and Cardiometabolic Indicators",
    x     = "Indicator status",
    y     = "Proportion with ≥14 mentally unhealthy days"
  )
```

> High mental health burden (≥14 mentally unhealthy days) varies
> substantially across physical and cardiometabolic indicators. The
> strongest associations are observed for indicators of functional and
> physical health limitations. Individuals reporting high physical
> health burden (≥14 physically unhealthy days) show a markedly elevated
> prevalence of high mental health burden ( ≈ 33%), compared with only
> about 6% among those without high physical burden. A similarly large
> difference appears for difficulty walking, where prevalence rises from
> roughly 7% to 24%.
>
> Cardiometabolic conditions such as diabetes, heart disease or
> myocardial infarction, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol are
> also associated with higher levels of mental health burden, although
> the magnitude of these differences is smaller. For example,
> respondents with diabetes or heart disease exhibit prevalence around
> 15%, compared with approximately 9% among those without these
> conditions.
>
> Lifestyle factors show a mixed pattern. Obesity is associated with
> moderately higher mental health burden (≈12% vs ≈8%), while physical
> activity appears protective, with prevalence roughly halved among
> individuals reporting recent physical activity ( ≈ 8%) compared with
> those reporting none ( ≈16%).
>
> Overall, the results suggest that mental health burden is most
> strongly linked with physical health limitations and functional
> impairment, while cardiometabolic disease and lifestyle factors
> exhibit weaker but still noticeable associations.

### 3.2.7 Summary of Key Findings

Several clear structural patterns emerge in the analysis of mental
health burden. First, the distribution of mentally unhealthy days is
highly right-skewed, with a large concentration at zero and a long tail
toward higher values. This indicates substantial heterogeneity in
psychological distress across the population and motivates the use of
count-based models with potential zero inflation in subsequent
modelling.

Second, strong **socioeconomic gradients** are visible. The prevalence
of high mental health burden declines consistently with increasing
income and education, suggesting that socioeconomic disadvantage is
associated with substantially higher levels of frequent psychological
distress.

Third, **demographic differences** are evident. Women report higher
average numbers of mentally unhealthy days than men across all age
groups, while mental health burden tends to decline gradually in older
age groups after midlife.

Fourth, mental and physical health burdens show **moderate
co-occurrence**, with higher levels of mentally unhealthy days tending
to coincide with higher levels of physically unhealthy days.

Finally, mental health burden is **most strongly associated with
indicators of physical limitation**, particularly poor physical health
and difficulty walking. Cardiometabolic conditions such as diabetes,
heart disease, hypertension, and high cholesterol also show positive
associations with mental health burden, although these relationships are
weaker than those linked to functional impairment.

Overall, the results suggest that mental health burden in this
population is structured primarily by **socioeconomic position, physical
health status, and functional limitation**, providing a clear empirical
basis for the modelling analyses developed in later sections.

### 3.2.8 Implications for Modelling

The exploratory analysis provides several insights relevant for model
development. The distribution of mentally unhealthy days is highly
right-skewed with a substantial concentration at zero, suggesting that
standard linear modelling would be inappropriate and motivating the use
of count-based models such as Poisson or negative binomial regression,
potentially with zero-inflation.

Socioeconomic gradients observed across income and education indicate
that demographic and socioeconomic variables should be included as key
predictors.

Finally, the strong co-occurrence between mental and physical health
indicators suggests that physical health variables such as poor physical
health days, difficulty walking, and chronic disease indicators may play
an important explanatory role in modelling mental health burden.

## 3.3 Predictor Association Structure and Multicollinearity Assessment

Before proceeding to multivariable modelling, it is essential to examine
the correlation structure among potential predictors. Strong pairwise
associations may indicate redundancy or multicollinearity, which can
inflate standard errors and complicate interpretation. This section
quantifies associations among continuous, ordinal, and binary variables
using Spearman's rank correlation and Cramér's V, respectively. The
analysis establishes which predictors are inter-related and informs
variable selection and multicollinearity diagnostics in subsequent
models.

### 3.3.1 Spearman Correlation Heatmap: Continuous and Ordinal Variables

```{r}
#| label: spearman-prep
#| include: false
corr_vars <- diab |>
  transmute(
    BMI,
    MentHlth,
    PhysHlth,
    GenHlth   = as.integer(GenHlth),
    Age       = as.integer(Age),
    Education = as.integer(Education),
    Income    = as.integer(Income)
  )

spear_mat <- cor(
  corr_vars,
  method = "spearman",
  use    = "pairwise.complete.obs"
)

spear_tidy <- spear_mat |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  rownames_to_column("Var1") |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = -Var1,
    names_to  = "Var2",
    values_to = "rho"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Var1 = factor(Var1, levels = colnames(spear_mat)),
    Var2 = factor(Var2, levels = colnames(spear_mat))
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: spearman-heatmap
#| fig.height: 7
#| fig.width: 10

ggplot(spear_tidy, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2, fill = rho)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", rho)),
    size   = 3.5,
    colour = "black"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "#2166AC",
    mid      = "white",
    high     = "#D6604D",
    midpoint = 0,
    limits   = c(-1, 1),
    name     = "Spearman's \u03c1"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Spearman rank correlations among continuous and ordinal variables",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = NULL
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 40, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y = element_text(hjust = 1)
  )
```

> The Spearman rank correlations indicate mostly **weak to moderate**
> monotonic associations among the continuous and ordinal variables.
>
> -   The strongest positive correlations are between **GenHlth and
>     PhysHlth** (ρ ≈ 0.45) and between **Education and Income** (ρ ≈
>     0.45), suggesting moderate monotonic relationships in both cases.
>
> -   **GenHlth** is moderately **negatively** correlated with
>     **Income** (ρ ≈ −0.35) and **Education** (ρ ≈ −0.28). Because
>     higher GenHlth scores denote poorer health, these negative
>     correlations imply that higher income and education are associated
>     with **better** self-rated health.
>
> -   **MentHlth** and **PhysHlth** show a modest positive association
>     (ρ ≈ 0.31), consistent with some co-occurrence of mental and
>     physical health burden.
>
> -   Most other pairwise correlations are small (\|ρ\| \< 0.3). In
>     particular, **BMI** and **Age** have only weak associations with
>     the other variables in this set.
>
> Overall, the correlation matrix does **not** indicate problematic
> collinearity: apart from a few moderate associations
> (GenHlth–PhysHlth, Education–Income), most variables are only weakly
> related. This supports their concurrent use as predictors in
> multivariable models, subject to routine multicollinearity checks.

### 3.3.2 Cramér's V Association Heatmap: Binary Clinical and Lifestyle Variables

To complement the Spearman correlation heatmap for continuous/ordinal
variables, the strength of pairwise associations among all binary
clinical and lifestyle indicators is quantified using Cramér's V. This
symmetric measure summarises the association in each 2×2 contingency
table on a 0–1 scale, without assuming any directionality. The analysis
establishes whether binary predictors cluster or operate independently,
informing whether multicollinearity diagnostics will be necessary in
subsequent regression models. Diabetes status is included here as a
predictor to assess its associations with other binary indicators.

```{r}
#| label: cramer-prep
#| include: false
bin_vars <- c("HighBP", "HighChol", "Smoker", "Stroke",
              "HeartDiseaseorAttack", "PhysActivity",
              "HvyAlcoholConsump", "DiffWalk",
              "AnyHealthcare", "NoDocbcCost","Diabetes")

cv_mat <- outer(
  bin_vars, bin_vars,
  Vectorize(function(a, b) {
    cramerV(table(diab[[a]], diab[[b]]))
  })
)
dimnames(cv_mat) <- list(bin_vars, bin_vars)

cv_tidy <- cv_mat |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  rownames_to_column("Var1") |>
  pivot_longer(-Var1, names_to = "Var2", values_to = "V") |>
  mutate(
    Var1 = factor(Var1, levels = bin_vars),
    Var2 = factor(Var2, levels = bin_vars)
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: cramerv-heatmap
#| fig.height: 7
#| fig.width: 10

ggplot(cv_tidy, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2, fill = V)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", V)),
    size   = 3.8,
    colour = "black"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low       = "white",
    high     = "#D6604D",
    midpoint = 0,
    limits   = c(0, 1),
    name     = "Cramér's V"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Cramér's V among binary clinical and lifestyle variables",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

> The Cramér’s V matrix indicates that most pairwise associations among
> the binary clinical and lifestyle variables are **weak**, with the
> majority of values below 0.20.
>
> The strongest association is between **HighBP and HighChol** (V ≈
> 0.30), representing a moderate relationship relative to the rest of
> the matrix. This is consistent with known clustering of cardiovascular
> risk factors. **Diabetes** shows its strongest associations with
> **HighBP** (V ≈ 0.27) and **DiffWalk** (V ≈ 0.22), followed by
> **HighChol** (V ≈ 0.21), indicating that diabetes tends to co-occur
> with cardiovascular risk factors and functional limitation.
>
> Other comparatively larger associations include:
>
> -   **PhysActivity and DiffWalk** (V ≈ 0.25)
>
> -   **HighBP and DiffWalk** (V ≈ 0.22)
>
> -   **HighBP and HeartDiseaseorAttack** (V ≈ 0.21)
>
> -   **Stroke and HeartDiseaseorAttack** (V ≈ 0.20)
>
> These moderate associations are clinically plausible and consistent
> with shared cardiovascular or functional impairment pathways.
>
> Lifestyle indicators such as **Smoker** and **HvyAlcoholConsump**
> exhibit generally weak associations with other variables (typically V
> \< 0.15). Healthcare access indicators (**AnyHealthcare**,
> **NoDocbcCost**) also show minimal association with most clinical
> variables.
>
> **Analytical Implication**
>
> The matrix does not suggest substantial redundancy among the binary
> predictors. Aside from a small number of moderate associations
> (primarily within cardiovascular-related variables and diabetes), most
> relationships are weak.

# 4. Refined Research Questions

## RQ1: Multidomain Determinants and Prediction of Diabetes Status

**Outcome Variable:** *Diabetes — Multiclass Outcome*

**Research Question**

> ***To what extent can diabetes status be explained and predicted using
> demographic, socioeconomic, behavioural, and cardiometabolic factors,
> and which predictors contribute most strongly to distinguishing
> between no diabetes, prediabetes, and diabetes?***

**Rationale**

Exploratory analysis of the CDC Diabetes Health Indicators dataset
indicates that diabetes status is structured by multiple interacting
determinants. Section 3.2.1 demonstrated a strong **age gradient**, with
prediabetes and diabetes concentrated in older age groups, suggesting
that demographic factors must be accounted for when evaluating other
risk indicators.

Cardiometabolic clustering was evident across several visualisations.
The joint distribution of **high blood pressure and high cholesterol**
increased substantially across diabetes categories, while the
risk-factor accumulation plot showed a clear gradient in which diabetes
prevalence rises with the number of cardiometabolic risk indicators.
This pattern aligns with established evidence linking obesity,
hypertension, and dyslipidaemia to type 2 diabetes risk (Ismail et al.,
2021).

Behavioural factors also exhibited variation across diabetes categories.
Physical activity levels tended to decline with worsening diabetes
status, while smoking and alcohol consumption showed weaker separation.
Because behavioural variables are potentially modifiable, evaluating
their contribution to diabetes risk is relevant for prevention
strategies (Aune et al., 2015).

Finally, socioeconomic gradients were visible in the education–income
distribution, where diabetes prevalence was higher among lower
socioeconomic groups. These patterns suggest that diabetes risk is
embedded within broader social determinants of health, which influence
exposure to risk factors and access to care (Hill-Briggs et al., 2021).

Together, these findings motivate a multivariable modelling approach to
estimate adjusted associations and evaluate predictive performance
across diabetes categories.

*'EDI Discussed in Task1'*

## RQ2: The Impact of Socioeconomic Moderation on Cardiovascular Risk

**Outcome Variables:** *HeartDiseaseorAttack* — binary outcome

**Research Question**

> ***To what extent do socioeconomic determinants, specifically income
> and education, moderate the impact of clinical risk factors on the
> prevalence of heart disease, and can the capture of complex feature
> interactions provide superior predictive insights compared to
> traditional additive assumptions?***

**Rationale**

Exploratory analysis identified distinct gradients in cardiovascular
health, where the prevalence of heart disease increases substantially
across older age groups and among individuals with specific clinical
markers. While factors such as high blood pressure, elevated
cholesterol, and body mass index are established universal risk
indicators, their influence is rarely isolated. In real-world clinical
contexts, these physiological markers exist within a broader
socioeconomic framework, suggesting that the "weight" of a clinical risk
factor may be compounded or mitigated by an individual's access to
resources, environmental stressors, and educational background (Roth et
al., 2024).

Traditional analytical frameworks often rely on the assumption that
risks are simply additive, yet biological and social systems frequently
exhibit synergistic effects where the presence of one factor
significantly alters the impact of another. For example, the
cardiovascular strain associated with high BMI may be intensified in
lower-income brackets due to limited access to preventive care or higher
cumulative stress (Collins et al., 2023). By investigating these
conditional relationships, this inquiry shifts the focus from simple
binary classification toward a more nuanced understanding of risk
stratification. This approach allows for the identification of specific
"risk profiles," which are intersections of clinical and social data
that might be overlooked by frameworks that fail to account for
higher-order interactions.

![The Dahlgren-Whitehead Rainbow Model illustrating the layers of health
determinants, providing the theoretical basis for the moderation
analysis of income and education in
RQ2.](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/SHAH-MEER/Modellin-Data/main/image/Rainbow-model-of-the-determinants-of-health.png){#fig-sdoh
fig-align="center" width="85%"}

As visualized in figure, individual clinical risks like blood pressure
and BMI are nested within broader socioeconomic environments. This
framework justifies the shift from simple predictive modelling to an
investigation of how these external layers moderate physiological risk.

**EDI and Ethical Considerations**

The application of predictive modelling in healthcare necessitates a
rigorous focus on transparency and equity. A primary ethical concern is
the risk of "algorithmic bias," where models may inadvertently penalise
or misrepresent specific subgroups if demographic variables are treated
in isolation. By specifically investigating socioeconomic moderators,
this research aims to move beyond a "one-size-fits-all" predictive
approach, ensuring that the influence of income and education is
explicitly understood rather than buried as "noise" within the data.

Furthermore, the interpretability of these complex interactions is a
matter of distributive justice, as clinicians must be able to explain
risk assessments to patients in a way that is both accurate and
contextually relevant. By quantifying how socioeconomic status shifts
the predictive significance of clinical markers, this study helps
identify whether existing healthcare inequalities are being reinforced
or challenged by the data. Ensuring that the model identifies these
non-linear patterns is essential for developing more equitable
preventative strategies that reflect the lived realities of diverse
population groups (Liu et al., 2023).

## RQ3: Socioeconomic and Lifestyle Determinants of Mental Health Burden

**Outcome Variable:** *MentHlth* — number of self-reported mentally
unhealthy days in the past 30 days (0–30)

**Research Question**

> *To what extent do socioeconomic position (income, education),
> lifestyle behaviours (physical activity, smoking), and physical health
> burden predict the number of mentally unhealthy days reported in the
> past 30 days, and do the protective associations of physical activity
> differ across income and education strata?*

**Rationale**

The *MentHlth* variable represents a count of mentally unhealthy days
within the past month (0–30). Exploratory analysis revealed a strongly
right-skewed distribution with a large concentration of zero values,
indicating that many respondents report no mentally unhealthy days while
a smaller subset experience substantial burden. Such distributions
violate assumptions of linear models and often produce overdispersion
and excess zeros, making standard Poisson or logistic approaches
unsuitable.

A Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) model is therefore appropriate
because it separates two processes:

1.  the probability that individuals experience no mental health burden,
    and

2.  the frequency of mentally unhealthy days among those who do report
    burden (Rust et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2023).

The EDA further revealed clear socioeconomic gradients in mental health
outcomes. Respondents in the lowest income categories exhibited
substantially higher prevalence of high mental health burden compared
with those in higher income groups. Physical activity also showed a
consistent inverse relationship with mentally unhealthy days, suggesting
potential behavioural protection.

These patterns motivate a modelling approach that jointly evaluates
socioeconomic position and lifestyle behaviours. In particular, testing
an interaction between income and physical activity allows examination
of whether the protective association of physical activity differs
across socioeconomic strata.

Prior research supports the relevance of these predictors. Socioeconomic
disadvantage has been consistently linked with elevated risk of mental
health disorders and poorer psychological well-being (Kessler et al.,
2010), while analyses of BRFSS data have shown that ZINB regression
provides an effective framework for modelling unhealthy-days indicators
with excess zeros (Rust et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2023).

**EDI / Ethical Considerations**

Socioeconomic gradients in mental health outcomes raise important equity
considerations. If income remains strongly associated with mental health
burden after adjustment for behavioural factors, this suggests that
structural determinants, such as economic insecurity and access to
resources, play a major role in shaping mental health outcomes.
Understanding these relationships can inform more equitable public
health strategies that address structural conditions rather than
focusing solely on individual behaviour.

# 6. Multidomain Determinants and Prediction of Diabetes Status

## 6.1 Modelling Objective

This section addresses the following research question:

> ***To what extent can diabetes status be explained and predicted using
> demographic, socioeconomic, behavioural, and cardiometabolic factors,
> and which predictors contribute most strongly to distinguishing
> between no diabetes, prediabetes, and diabetes?***

The objective is twofold:

1.  **Explanatory** – to estimate the adjusted associations between
    predictor domains and diabetes status.

2.  **Predictive** – to compare the classification performance of
    statistical and machine learning models.

Diabetes status is treated as a **multiclass outcome** (No diabetes /
Prediabetes / Diabetes), requiring modelling approaches suitable for
nominal categorical outcomes.

## 6.2. Outcome Variable

The dependent variable is **diabetes status**, categorised into:

-   **No diabetes**

-   **Prediabetes**

-   **Diabetes.**

Exploratory analysis demonstrated class imbalance, with the majority of
respondents in the no-diabetes category and substantially smaller
proportions in the prediabetes and diabetes groups. This imbalance is
accounted for in model training and evaluation.

## 6.3 Training and Validation

### 6.3.1 **Data Partitioning and Resampling:**

The dataset is partitioned into a 75% training set and a 25% testing set
using stratified sampling on diabetes status. Within the training set,
5-fold stratified cross-validation is implemented to estimate model
performance during development. The held-out test set is reserved for
final evaluation to ensure unbiased performance assessment.

```{r}
set.seed(42)

diabetes_split <- initial_split(
  diab,
  prop = 0.75,
  strata = Diabetes
)

diab_train <- training(diabetes_split)
diab_test  <- testing(diabetes_split)


diabetes_folds <- vfold_cv(
  diab_train,
  v = 5,
  strata = Diabetes
)

# Sanity checks
diab_train |>  count(Diabetes) |> mutate(prop = n/sum(n))
diab_test |> count(Diabetes) |>  mutate(prop = n/sum(n))

```

### 6.3.2 Recipe Definition and Preprocessing Strategy

-   **Outcome specification** – Models `Diabetes` as a three-level
    categorical outcome (No diabetes, Prediabetes, Diabetes).

-   **Near-zero variance removal** – Removes predictors with minimal
    variability to prevent instability and redundant information.

-   **Ordinal scoring** – Converts ordered factors (`Age`, `Education`,
    `Income`, `GenHlth`) into numeric scores while preserving their
    natural ranking. This introduces an assumption of approximately
    equal spacing between adjacent categories; however, the primary
